title: Round-robin endpoint selection in connect_with_failover()
description:
What: Modify connect_with_failover() to use round-robin starting point instead of always trying endpoints[0] first, to distribute load across healthy endpoints.

Algorithm:
1. In wallet/src/client.rs, add a module-level AtomicUsize counter (ENDPOINT_COUNTER) initialized to 0
2. In connect_with_failover(), before the loop: start_idx = ENDPOINT_COUNTER.fetch_add(1, Relaxed) % endpoints.len()
3. Iterate endpoints starting at start_idx, wrapping around via modular arithmetic
4. Behavior is identical to existing sequential failover when only 1 endpoint exists

Defensive programming rules:
- Use std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize with Relaxed ordering (no cross-thread happens-before needed for a counter)
- Guard against empty endpoints slice before modulo (existing check already handles this)
- Do not use a Mutex or RwLock (overkill for a counter)
- The function signature does not change; callers are unaffected

Agent team: R (read connect_with_failover() exact implementation) + I (add atomic counter, restructure loop) + T (test that starting index rotates on successive calls)

Tests:
- test name: round_robin_rotates_starting_endpoint — oracle: call connect_with_failover with 3 mock endpoints, capture which was tried first each call; assert index cycles 0,1,2,0,...
- Note: this requires a mock or test-double for connection attempts
acceptance_criteria: cargo test --package nie-wallet -- client::tests passes; with multiple endpoints the starting index rotates across calls; cargo clippy -D warnings clean
